food_subgroup: Mushroom-based dishes; marinated and grilled vegetable preparations

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for Malai Kumbh Tikka. FOODON_03302114 (mushroom food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers prepared mushroom products; used as closest parent as no specific tikka preparation code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Malai Kumbh Tikka or equivalent Indian mushroom tikka preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Malai Kumbh Tikka is approximately 200 g, comprising 6–8 marinated mushroom pieces served as a starter or side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Mushroom (button, raw) and cream-based marinade components; ICMR-NIN composite estimation for malai tikka preparation
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Malai Kumbh Tikka as a composite dish. Values are estimated from ICMR-NIN data for button mushrooms (raw), fresh cream (malai), yogurt, and spice components, weighted by typical recipe proportions (approx. 60% mushroom, 25% cream/yogurt marinade, 15% spices and oil). Cooking losses for grilling/tandoor estimated at ~15% moisture reduction. Values cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entries for button mushrooms (FDC ID 169251) and heavy cream (FDC ID 170859).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Mushrooms (cooked/prepared); analogous estimate for cream-marinated mushroom tikka
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Malai Kumbh Tikka. GI estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked mushrooms (GI ~15, low category). The cream and yogurt marinade components have negligible glycemic contribution. The overall dish GI is dominated by the mushroom base and is conservatively estimated at 15. GL per 200 g serving = (15 × 5.8 × 200/100) / 100 = 1.74, rounded to 1.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from marinade spices and salt (~310 mg/100 g); a 200 g serving provides approximately 620 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a full restaurant serving of Malai Kumbh Tikka can contribute approximately 27% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit; requesting reduced salt in the marinade is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15) and low net carbohydrate content (~5.8 g/100 g); minimal postprandial glucose impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Malai Kumbh Tikka is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a starter due to its very low GI and low carbohydrate load; however, the cream-based marinade adds saturated fat and calories that should be accounted for in overall dietary planning.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from fresh cream (malai) marinade (~4.5 g/100 g); a 200 g serving provides approximately 9 g saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size of Malai Kumbh Tikka, as the cream-based marinade contributes significant saturated fat; choosing a yogurt-only marinade variant or limiting to a half-portion can reduce saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Malai Kumbh Tikka is a popular North Indian vegetarian appetiser made from button mushrooms (kumbh) marinated in a rich, creamy mixture of malai (fresh cream), yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, green chilli, cumin, garam masala, kasuri methi (dried fenugreek leaves), and lemon juice. The marinated mushrooms are skewered and cooked in a tandoor (clay oven) or on a grill until lightly charred on the outside while remaining tender and juicy inside. The dish has a mild, creamy, subtly spiced flavour profile with a smoky aroma imparted by the tandoor. Malai Kumbh Tikka is commonly served as a starter or snack at Indian restaurants, accompanied by mint chutney and sliced onions. Nutritionally, it is relatively low in carbohydrates and has a very low glycemic index (~15), making it a suitable option for individuals managing blood glucose. The cream-based marinade contributes moderate saturated fat and calories (~112 kcal/100 g). Sodium content is moderate (~310 mg/100 g) due to added salt and spices. The dish is naturally gluten-free and vegetarian. In Singapore, it is served at Indian restaurants as a premium starter, often priced around SGD 18–19 per portion.

ingredient_synonyms.tandoor: traditional clay oven used in Indian cooking

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN or HPB entry exists for Malai Kumbh Tikka as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN component data for button mushrooms, fresh cream, and yogurt, weighted by typical recipe proportions and adjusted for tandoor cooking losses. GI is estimated by analogy from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked mushrooms. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are approved and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from ICMR-NIN data for button mushrooms, fresh cream (malai), and yogurt, cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central. No single approved-source entry for this exact dish exists. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for mushroom-based preparations; note that some mushroom varieties can contain vitamin D depending on UV exposure, but this is not quantified for the restaurant preparation. 3. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked mushrooms; no direct GI measurement for Malai Kumbh Tikka exists in the literature. 4. Sodium estimate of 310 mg/100 g reflects typical restaurant-level salting; home preparations may vary significantly. 5. Trans fat is estimated at 0.1 g/100 g reflecting trace amounts from dairy cream; this is within normal dairy trans fat ranges and is not industrially added. 6. The food is served at Dugout - Circular Road, Singapore, at SGD 18.80 per portion. 7. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this specific Indian mushroom tikka preparation.
